text: Neuroscientist Tali Sharot's research on the 'optimism bias' shows that the human brain systematically overestimates positive future outcomes. Social media exploits this bias in reverse: the feed presents others' best outcomes — the promotion, the vacation, the engagement — and our pattern-matching brains generalize from peaks to averages. We do not think 'my friend had one good trip'; we update our model of their life as uniformly excellent. This is not stupidity; it is a predictable feature of a brain designed for small-group inference being exposed to curated large-scale data. The mismatch between brain architecture and feed design is the engine of social comparison anxiety.
question: What cognitive mechanism does the passage identify as the engine of social comparison anxiety on social media?

options.text: The optimism bias causes users to believe their own lives will eventually match those they see online.
The brain generalizes from others' highlighted peak moments to assume their entire lives are uniformly excellent.
Mirror neurons cause users to feel the emotions displayed in posts as if they were their own.
Sharot found that dopamine released during scrolling prevents accurate self-assessment.
